title: Companion Model
one_liner: Split high-judgement planning or verification from bounded execution, and choose model size for each role.
problem: A single model must simultaneously keep the task model, make architectural judgements, operate tools and verify output. Running the largest model for every step is expensive; assigning every step to a small model can hide planning and review mistakes.
solution: Use a large model to plan, decompose and review while a small, tool-bounded executor implements well-specified steps; where drafts are cheap and ambiguity is high, invert the split: let a small model draft and a large model verify or repair. Pass explicit artefacts between roles and use tests, linters or review criteria as the acceptance signal. Treat this as a per-task-class choice, not a default for agentic coding: it helps when roles have measurably different capability needs and hand-offs are cheap; it loses when coordination latency, context transfer or review overhead exceeds the saving.
